73: **South Africa**, runners-up in **Group A** on 4 points behind perfect **Mexico**, meet **Canada**, who advanced as **Group B**'s second seed on the same total behind **Switzerland**. Both arrive with identical 1W-1D-1L records and now headline the tournament's first knockout tie — a 12:00 PM PT meeting where extra time and penalties loom if the 90 settles nothing.
67: **Panama**, fourth in **Group L** on 0 points, face **England**, who sit on 4 points and need a result to confirm their place in the knockouts. **England** will be without right-back Reece James, who has been ruled out with a hamstring injury sustained against **Ghana** and didn't travel with the squad to New Jersey. The good news for Thomas Tuchel is that Bukayo Saka — who has been managing an Achilles tendinitis issue that hampered his end-of-season form at Arsenal — has been declared fully fit and is expected to start for the first time since the Champions League final on May 30. Declan Rice is walking a yellow-card tightrope: one more booking and he'll be suspended for the Round of 32. **Panama** are pointless and eliminated, but that makes them dangerous — nothing to lose, an adoring crowd of travelling fans, and every reason to throw caution to the wind. As Men in Blazers previewed on their Night Cup show, Harry Kane is on the verge of history: one more goal would make him **England**'s all-time leading scorer at major tournaments, surpassing Gary Lineker.
68: **Croatia**, third in **Group L** on 3 points, take on **Ghana** in a winner-takes-all clash at Lincoln Financial Field. **Croatia** must win to guarantee their place in the knockouts; anything less and they'll need other results to cooperate. Manager Zlatko Dalic is expected to start Ante Budimir — the decisive substitute who scored the only goal in the 1-0 win over **Panama** — from the opening whistle. Luka Modric will earn his 201st cap for **Croatia**, extending his remarkable international career, with Mateo Kovacic alongside him in midfield. **Ghana** know a draw may be enough to progress, but they'll be without first-choice goalkeeper Lawrence Ati-Zigi, who has been ruled out of the rest of the tournament with a groin injury. Benjamin Asare, who kept a clean sheet against **England**, steps in between the posts. Men in Blazers spotlighted this as one of the day's must-watch matches on their Night Cup preview — and with both sides' tournament lives on the line in a simultaneous kick-off alongside **Panama** vs **England**, **Group L**'s finale should deliver pure drama.
71: **Colombia**, top of **Group K** on a perfect 6 points, clash with **Portugal**, second on 4, in a blockbuster top-two showdown at Hard Rock Stadium in Miami that will decide the group winner and the more favourable knockout path. **Colombia** can seal first place with a draw, but manager Nestor Lorenzo will be managing the fitness of veteran playmaker James Rodriguez, who was substituted after 58 minutes against **Congo DR** and has had his minutes carefully managed. **Portugal** need a win to leapfrog to the top, and Cristiano Ronaldo arrives in red-hot form after a brace in the 5-0 demolition of **Uzbekistan** — a performance that silenced questions about his place in the starting XI after a quiet goalless draw with **Congo DR** in the opener. Defender Tomas Araujo, who missed the **Uzbekistan** match with a knock, has returned to training and appears available. Men in Blazers dedicated a segment of their Night Cup preview to this one, framing it as the group stage's marquee farewell: Ronaldo chasing glory, **Colombia**'s golden generation asserting themselves, and bracket implications that could ripple all the way to the semifinals.
72: **Congo DR**, third in **Group K** on 1 point, face **Uzbekistan**, bottom on 0 points and already eliminated — but this match carries real consequence for the Leopards. A win would lift **Congo DR** to 4 points and, depending on results across the other groups, could be enough to advance as one of the eight best third-placed teams. **Congo DR** have been one of the tournament's most impressive defensive units, holding **Portugal** to a goalless draw and losing just 1-0 to group leaders **Colombia**, and they should be heavy favourites against an **Uzbekistan** side that has conceded 8 goals in two matches, including a 5-0 drubbing by **Portugal**. This is the first ever competitive meeting between the two nations. For **Uzbekistan**, this is a farewell appearance at their first-ever finals — pride and individual performances are all that's left to play for.
69: **Algeria** and **Austria** meet in a straight shootout for the second knockout berth in **Group J**, with **Argentina** already confirmed as group winners. Both sides sit on 3 points with near-identical records, and the math is simple: win and you're almost certainly through, lose and you're heading home, draw and you're sweating on goal difference and third-place calculations. **Algeria** face a major blow up front — striker Mohamed Amoura, who picked up a hamstring injury in the defeat to **Argentina** on June 17, has continued to train individually but hasn't rejoined full team sessions and is listed as questionable. If he can't go, **Algeria** lose their most dynamic attacking threat. **Austria** defender Stefan Posch will play wearing a protective face mask after suffering a broken jaw in the opening match against **Jordan** — he flew to Los Angeles twice to get fitted for a custom brace and has played through it since. **Algeria** enter on the back of a gutsy 2-1 comeback win over **Jordan**, sealed by Amine Gouiri's late strike, while **Austria** are licking their wounds from a 2-0 defeat to **Argentina** in which Lionel Messi scored twice.
70: **Jordan**, bottom of **Group J** on 0 points and already eliminated after back-to-back defeats, face an **Argentina** side that has already clinched top spot with a perfect 6 points. This will be the first ever meeting between the two nations, and manager Lionel Scaloni has confirmed he'll rotate heavily — Lionel Messi is not expected to start, giving fringe players a chance to audition ahead of the knockouts. Even a much-changed **Argentina** side will have far too much quality on paper, with Julian Alvarez likely to lead the line and capable of punishing any defensive lapse. For **Jordan**, this is their last chance to earn a first-ever point at a major tournament finals, and forward Mousa Tamari — their most dangerous outlet with his pace and directness — will be desperate to make an impression against a reshuffled back line. The atmosphere at AT&T Stadium should be special regardless of the stakes: **Jordan**'s travelling fans have been among the most passionate of the tournament, and **Argentina**'s support needs no introduction.
